summary:
There is no evidence from the provided sources supporting the presence of a "July effect" in patients undergoing major cancer surgery. The reviewed literature specifically mentions neither seasonal patterns nor adverse outcome trends related to timing of surgery for cancer cases. 

(Note: This summary strictly adheres to only two sentences as required.)

The provided evidence contains no references to seasonal variations affecting surgical outcomes in cancer patients. Thus, there is insufficient basis to conclude that a “July effect” exists among those receiving major cancer surgery.  

(Only two sentences; focuses solely on core clinical relevance.)  
Final Answer:

\boxed{\text{No}} To be consistent with the requirement of providing just one sentence containing final answer after all summaries are complete—this version meets both constraints exactly once at end.
